disclaimer: Manual end-of-day snapshot compiled from public sources for informational purposes only — not validated for trading or investment decisions.
marketPulse: Intel's Best Day Since 1987 Drives Nasdaq and S&P to Records; Oil Yo-Yos on Iran Peace Hopes
marketPulseSubtext: The S&P 500 rose 0.80% to a record 7,165 and the Nasdaq surged 1.63% to 24,837 as Intel's 23.6% post-earnings surge lifted chips to an 18th consecutive winning day. Oil whipsawed between $103 and $107 Brent before fading on Iran diplomacy signals, while the Dow slipped 0.2% as software and industrials lagged. Consumer sentiment hit an all-time record low of 49.8 but the stock market shrugged it off entirely.
topDriver: Intel's strongest earnings day since 1987 ignited a semiconductor sweep that overwhelmed the rest of the tape
narrativeSummary: April 24 was a semiconductors-versus-everything-else session. Intel's blowout Q1 beat — its sixth consecutive earnings outperformance — sent shares up 23.6% for their best day since the dot-com era, dragging AMD +14%, Qualcomm +11%, and SOXX to an unprecedented 18-day win streak. The Nasdaq hit fresh records while the Dow fell, a stark divergence that highlights how narrow the rally's leadership has become. Oil added a second layer of drama: Brent swung between $103 and $107 before closing around $104.40 as Iran's foreign minister headed to Islamabad for potential peace talks, providing a fleeting relief valve on crude. Bonds cooperated — the 10Y slipped to 4.31% from 4.34% — offering a mild tailwind to tech multiples. The one jarring data point was Michigan consumer sentiment printing at a record-low 49.8 with 1-year inflation expectations jumping to 4.7%, but equity investors chose to look through the consumer gloom and focus on AI earnings momentum instead. The session's key tension: record stock highs coexisting with record-low consumer confidence and a Strait of Hormuz still closed.
macroStressSubtext: Oil volatility and record-low sentiment offset by semi earnings surge and easing yields
rateCurveRead: Yields fell modestly across the curve on April 24 — the 10Y eased to 4.31% from 4.34% and the 2Y dipped to 3.78% — as Iran peace-talk optimism briefly reduced the energy-driven inflation premium. The curve shape is still bear-steepened with short end anchored by Fed-on-hold expectations and the long end pricing a persistent energy-inflation tail risk. The mild easing was a minor tailwind to tech multiples rather than a regime shift.

scenarios.label: Bear
scenarios.title: Peace Talks Collapse, Chips Crack
scenarios.trigger: Islamabad talks fail, Brent surges back above $110, SOXX 18-day streak breaks hard, VIX clears 22
scenarios.outcome: Semiconductor leadership reversal would remove the market's only reliable bid. Oil resurgence would re-ignite inflation concerns, pressure tech multiples, and force a re-pricing of Fed path. Broad selloff with IWM and consumer stocks most exposed.

scenarios.label: Base
scenarios.title: Chips Lead, Oil Stays Volatile, Records Hold Narrowly
scenarios.trigger: Partial Iran diplomatic progress keeps oil in $100-108 range, semi earnings continue beating, VIX stays 17-21
scenarios.outcome: Records extend but on increasingly thin leadership — mega-cap tech and semis do the work while software, consumer, and small caps lag. Consumer sentiment weakness starts leaking into guidance language but doesn't derail AI capex cycle. Sideways-to-higher tape with high dispersion.

scenarios.label: Bull
scenarios.title: Iran Deal Unlocks Broad Rally
scenarios.trigger: Islamabad talks produce credible ceasefire path, Brent drops toward $90, yields ease further, software recovers
scenarios.outcome: Oil shock premium collapses, inflation fears recede, bonds rally alongside equities. Breadth broadens dramatically — IWM, software, consumer discretionary all catch up. Fed rate cut expectations return, gold rebounds as haven bid rotates. S&P pushes toward 7,400.

topThemes.theme: Intel surged 23.6% — its best day since 1987 — on a Q1 earnings blowout and raised AI CPU guidance
topThemes.impact: SOXX, QQQ, Nasdaq, AMD, Qualcomm, NVDA, AI capex narrative

topThemes.impact: Brent, WTI, energy stocks, inflation expectations, consumer

topThemes.theme: Consumer sentiment hit an all-time record low of 49.8 with 1-year inflation expectations at 4.7%
topThemes.impact: Consumer discretionary, Fed path, retail, K-shaped divergence narrative

topThemes.theme: Nasdaq-Dow divergence sharpened — tech records while software and industrials lagged badly

topThemes.theme: Kevin Warsh DOJ probe cleared — path to Fed chair confirmation opens, hawkish signal for rates
topThemes.impact: Fed path pricing, 2Y Treasury, rate-sensitive sectors

crossMarket.driver: SPY closed at $713.33 near session highs — a new all-time closing high — though the majority of index constituents fell, with the gain almost entirely driven by semiconductor weight.
QQQ closed at $663.88, up from $651.42, for a 1.9% gain led entirely by chip stocks. The index hit a fresh all-time high.
DIA slipped ~0.2% as software, Comcast (-8% on downgrade), and industrial names lagged the chip-driven tape.
IWM added roughly 0.3%, a modest catch-up but still sharply underperforming Nasdaq. Small caps remain hamstrung by rate sensitivity and lack of AI exposure.
SOXX surged 4.67% to $462.16 on Intel's blowout and AMD's 14% pop — an 18th consecutive winning session, the longest streak ever recorded for the index.

Intel surged 23.6% after its sixth consecutive earnings beat — Q1 revenue of $13.58B smashed the $12.42B consensus. CEO Lip-Bu Tan highlighted AI-centric CPU demand surge. Best single-day gain since 1987.
Brent whipsawed between $103-$107 before closing around $104.40 — down slightly on the day — as Iran's FM Araghchi headed to Islamabad for peace talks, providing a brief relief valve.
WTI closed around $94.88, down from $95.85, as diplomatic signals eased the supply-fear premium modestly. Still on track for ~14% weekly gain.
The 10Y yield eased to 4.31% from 4.34% as Iran peace-talk optimism took a small amount of inflation risk premium out of the long end.
The 2Y eased to 3.78% — the Warsh Fed confirmation news is hawkish on structure but near-term Fed-on-hold expectations keep the front end anchored.
Gold recovered to $4,723 after approaching $4,700 earlier in the week. A potential Iran deal reduces haven demand but inflation expectations at 4.7% provide a floor.
VIX eased to ~18.76-18.92 — near post-Iran-war lows — as the long/short-vol dispersion trade continues. Structurally fragile: any shock could trigger rapid short-covering.
Bitcoin rose to $78,594, up 4%, moving in sympathy with the risk-on tech tone — continuing its pattern of tracking AI sentiment rather than acting as an independent haven.
Software stocks extended Thursday's SaaSpocalypse selloff — IBM, ServiceNow, Workday, and Microsoft all fell. IGV is now down nearly 30% since last fall, a sharp divergence from chip stocks.

drivers.name: Intel's Q1 earnings blowout — 6th straight beat, AI CPU demand surge, best day since 1987
drivers.detail: Intel posted Q1 revenue of $13.58B vs $12.42B expected and raised guidance on AI-centric CPU demand. CEO Lip-Bu Tan cited a massive surge in data-center CPU orders. The stock surged 23.6% — its largest single-day gain since the dot-com era — dragging AMD +14%, Qualcomm +11%, and extending SOXX's streak to 18 sessions.
drivers.linked: INTC, AMD, QCOM, SOXX, QQQ, AI capex

drivers.name: Iran FM heading to Islamabad — oil yo-yo relief valve but Hormuz still closed
drivers.detail: Iranian Foreign Minister Araghchi traveled to Pakistan with reports of a 'high likelihood of breakthrough' in US-Iran negotiations. Brent, which had been above $106, swung down toward $103 before settling ~$104.40. The Strait of Hormuz remains closed and the naval blockade intact — this was a sentiment shift, not a fundamental change.
drivers.linked: Brent, WTI, energy stocks, inflation expectations

drivers.name: Michigan consumer sentiment hit all-time record low of 49.8; 1-year inflation expectations jumped to 4.7%
drivers.detail: The final April Michigan sentiment reading was revised up slightly to 49.8 from the preliminary 47.6 but remains the weakest in the survey's history. Year-ahead inflation expectations surged to 4.7%, the largest monthly jump since April 2025, while long-term expectations rose to 3.5%. The stock market ignored this entirely, highlighting the K-shaped divergence between asset prices and consumer stress.
drivers.linked: Consumer discretionary, Fed path, retail, household spending
drivers.source: University of Michigan / TradingEconomics / Advisor Perspectives

drivers.name: Kevin Warsh DOJ probe cleared — Fed chair path open, hawkish rate structure signal
drivers.detail: The Justice Department ended its investigation into Jerome Powell, removing the roadblock for Sen. Tillis to support Warsh's confirmation. Warsh has pledged Fed independence but is viewed as hawkish on balance sheet normalization. Markets see this as a longer-term headwind to rate-cut expectations.
drivers.linked: Fed path, 2Y Treasury, rate-sensitive sectors, USD

risks.title: Semiconductor concentration: the entire market is riding an 18-day chip streak — any crack is systemic
risks.desc: SOXX's RSI hit 85 — a level that has historically preceded corrections. NewEdge Wealth warns the rally is 'dangerously reliant on cyclical semiconductors.' If Intel's or SOXX's momentum breaks, there is limited breadth elsewhere to absorb it — software is already down 30% from its highs.

risks.title: Consumer-market divergence is at historic extremes and must eventually resolve
risks.desc: Record-low consumer sentiment of 49.8 alongside record-high S&P 500 levels is a historically unusual configuration. Consumer spending accounts for ~70% of GDP. If gasoline prices stay above $4/gallon and the Iran shock persists, spending data will eventually reflect sentiment, and guidance will follow.

risks.title: Iran peace talks remain fragile — Hormuz closed, blockade intact
risks.desc: Today's diplomatic signal is Iran's FM traveling to Pakistan — it is not a deal. The Strait of Hormuz remains closed, the US naval blockade of Iranian ports is active, and Iran has not submitted a formal proposal. A single breakdown in Islamabad could send Brent back toward $110 within hours.

risks.title: VIX at post-war lows while realized single-stock vol is ferocious — dispersion trap risk
risks.desc: VIX near 18.76 reflects a long-stock / short-vol positioning trade that Bloomberg describes as an 'important marker of complacency.' Intel +24% and Comcast -8% on the same day shows realized vol is high. A concentrated shock could trigger rapid VIX short-covering, as seen in early 2018 when VIX briefly spiked above 50.

risks.title: Software sector is in a bear market that the headline indices are hiding
risks.desc: IGV-proxy software stocks are down nearly 30% since last fall. IBM, ServiceNow, Workday, and Intuit all sold off this week. This is a genuine sector recession happening underneath record Nasdaq headline numbers — if it spreads to AI infrastructure names, the breadth story collapses.

timeline.headline: Intel reported Q1 results after Thursday's close — $13.58B revenue vs $12.42B expected, with raised guidance on AI CPU demand. Stock surged 27% pre-market.
timeline.effect: Set up a chip-led gap higher for Nasdaq and QQQ; Dow futures were mixed as the move was entirely sector-specific.

timeline.headline: Markets opened with Nasdaq +0.7%, S&P +0.3%, Dow flat. SOXX gap opened toward new all-time highs. AMD, Qualcomm, ASML, TSMC all up 3-12% in sympathy.
timeline.effect: Tech leadership was broad within semis but didn't extend to software or industrials, widening the intra-market divergence.

timeline.headline: Michigan consumer sentiment final print: 49.8 (record low) with 1-year inflation expectations at 4.7%. Oil briefly spiked toward $107 Brent on residual Hormuz tension.
timeline.effect: Markets shrugged the sentiment miss entirely. Oil's spike was short-lived as Iran diplomat travel news hit wires.

timeline.headline: Iran FM Araghchi confirmed heading to Islamabad for peace talks. Trump told Reuters Iran 'plans to make an offer.' Brent pulled back to $104.40 from $107.
timeline.effect: Oil relief reduced the inflation tail slightly, helping the 10Y ease to 4.31% and providing a secondary boost to tech multiples.

timeline.headline: S&P 500 closed at 7,165 (+0.80%) and Nasdaq at 24,837 (+1.63%) — both records. Dow fell 0.2%. SOXX extended win streak to 18 days. VIX closed ~18.92.
timeline.effect: Session confirmed the AI earnings cycle is overriding macro headwinds — but the Dow/Nasdaq split shows how narrow the leadership base has become.

timeline.headline: Islamabad talks outcome this weekend. Hyperscalers report next week — Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, Apple all due. FOMC meeting May 6-7.
timeline.effect: Weekend Iran developments are binary for Monday open. Hyperscaler earnings will either validate or stress-test the AI capex thesis that is carrying the entire market.

watchpoints.event: Islamabad US-Iran talks outcome — deal or breakdown binary for oil and equities on Monday open
Microsoft Q1 earnings — first hyperscaler to report, sets tone for AI capex cycle validation
Meta Q1 earnings — AI ad spend and capex guidance critical for semi demand chain
Amazon Q1 earnings — AWS cloud capex and consumer health signal
Q1 GDP advance estimate — first read on economic growth against a backdrop of record consumer gloom
Apple Q1 earnings + PCE inflation data — consumer health from both angles on the same day
FOMC meeting — no rate change expected but Warsh confirmation path and Iran inflation shock will shape language

strategies.title: SOXX Streak Continuation vs. RSI Reversal Watch
strategies.body: SOXX at RSI 85 with an 18-day win streak is historically overbought but momentum is real. The trade is whether hyperscaler earnings (MSFT, META, AMZN next week) validate AI capex spending that justifies chip demand. A beat extends the streak; a miss or cautious capex guidance is the first serious threat.
strategies.trigger: SOXX holds above $462 and hyperscaler earnings confirm AI capex is accelerating

strategies.title: Iran Deal Binary: Oil Relief Trade
strategies.body: A credible Islamabad framework would take $8-12 off Brent rapidly, easing inflation expectations, unlocking a bond rally, and broadening equity leadership to consumer and rate-sensitive sectors. The setup is clean but the catalyst is binary and weekend-dependent.
strategies.trigger: Islamabad talks produce a formal ceasefire framework with Hormuz reopening timeline

strategies.title: Software Sector Recovery Watch
strategies.body: Software is down ~30% from its highs while the Nasdaq hits records — the valuation gap versus semis is near historic extremes. A recovery requires either an earnings beat from a bellwether (Salesforce, ServiceNow, Workday) or an oil-driven rate relief that re-rates growth multiples.
strategies.trigger: A major software name beats and raises; or 10Y falls below 4.15% on Iran relief

strategies.title: Warsh Hawkish Regime — Short Long-Duration Assets
strategies.body: Warsh's confirmation path is now clear. His hawkish balance-sheet stance suggests long-term yields may not fall as much as the market expects even if the Fed cuts short rates. Long-duration tech and real estate remain structurally exposed.
strategies.trigger: Warsh Senate confirmation proceeds; 30Y holds above 4.85% despite any near-term easing

strategies.title: Consumer Sentiment vs. Spending Divergence — Eventual Snap
strategies.body: Record-low consumer sentiment at 49.8 with 4.7% 1-year inflation expectations will eventually show up in spending data. Retail, restaurants, discretionary travel, and auto-adjacent names are the most exposed. The question is when — currently the stock market is pricing in that it never happens.
strategies.trigger: PCE (May 1) or NFP (May 8) show consumer spending weakening below expectations
